title: Replacement ewe selection and retention
subtitle: Choosing which ewe lambs to keep and which to sell.
excerpt: A productive small flock replaces 15,20% of its ewes each year. Keeping back the right lambs as replacements, choosing for mothering ability, body condition, feet, and breed fit, is how the flock improves over time. Keeping the wrong ones is how it declines.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb, Ewe Replacement and Culling Guide. DEFRA/APHA Sheep Welfare Code. SAC Consulting Scotland, Flock Health and Productivity.

glossaryTerms.definition: A young female sheep that has been weaned but not yet had her first lamb; between weaning and her first mating season, she is known as a gimmer or ewe lamb.
A numerical score from 1 (very thin) to 5 (very fat) used to assess the fat cover and muscle of a sheep by feel over the spine and short ribs.

content.text: Ewe replacement is the main lever you have for flock improvement. Every ewe lamb kept back that doesn't earn her keep, because she loses condition, has bad feet, produces little milk, or loses her lambs, costs you a year's grass and feed for a negative return. The selection decisions at weaning in July and August set the flock's trajectory for the next five or six years.
Aim to replace 15,20% of the ewe flock each year. In a flock of 20 ewes, that means keeping back three to four

from this year's lambs, assuming you're not selling the whole crop. If your flock size is stable and replacements come from within the flock, you need a roughly 100% lambing percentage (one lamb per ewe) to sustain the replacement pool while selling surplus.
Body size and condition: choose lambs that reached target weaning weight on the dam's milk alone. A lamb that struggles to wean at condition score 2 will be a poor producer.
Udder and feet in the dam: the most heritable traits in a ewe are feet and udder quality. Don't keep lambs from a ewe with a broken-down udder or persistent foot problems.
Temperament: a lamb that is constantly panicking or breaking fences will pass this onto her lambs. Select for calm stock.
Structure: four sound legs, a level back, no obvious structural defects. Run your hands over the spine and loin, a
of 3 or above at weaning is a reasonable minimum.

Cull on these grounds: three or more teeth broken or missing; a one-sided or broken udder; foot problems that return every season; or a ewe that loses condition every winter no matter how well she's fed. A ewe that loses a lamb twice in a row through poor mothering is also a cull candidate.

Breeding your own replacements gives you known health history and animals adapted to your land. Buying in works when your lambing percentage is too low to produce your own, or when you want to bring in a different breed character. Any bought-in sheep triggers the 6-day movement standstill; this needs to be factored into your autumn calendar.
